projects.title: I Automated My Job Hunt
projects.tagline: An end-to-end AI pipeline that automates the tedious parts of job hunting.

hook.heading: I Automated My Job Hunt
hook.paragraph: Job hunting is tedious in a way that's hard to explain until you're in it. Every application is roughly the same process: find a listing, read through it, research the company, figure out if it's worth your time, tailor your CV, write a cover letter. Repeated dozens of times with almost no feedback on whether any of it is working. At some point I stopped asking how to do it better and started asking how much of it I could just stop doing myself.

problem.heading: The problem
problem.paragraph: The hardest part isn't any single application. It's the fact that each one demands the same level of care and attention as the last. Reading a job description properly takes time. Researching a company well takes time. Writing something that doesn't sound generic takes time. None of it is difficult exactly, but all of it together, repeated over and over, starts to feel like it's eating your life. And the worst part is that most of it is the same work, done slightly differently, every single time.

pipeline.heading: Pipeline overview
pipeline.intro: Here's what happens from the moment a search runs to a tailored application landing in the dashboard.

steps.name: Scrape
steps.description: Pulls listings from job boards and writes them to the database.

steps.name: Screen
steps.description: A fast, cheap pass that scores every job.

steps.name: Analyse
steps.description: A deep dive on the jobs you pick.

steps.name: Tailor
steps.description: Rewrites your CV and generates a cover letter targeted to the role.

parts.intro: This isn't really a timeline. It's a handful of decisions, the good calls and the ones I had to take back, and the reasoning behind both.
paragraphs: The original plan was to be clever about cost. When you lack experience with LLMs one of the things you hear about a lot are the costs. Tokens can be expensive, and so heading into this project I knew keeping costs down was something I wanted to try very hard at, so when I was designing the first version of this project I used a multi-model approach. Using paid Sonnet 4.6 calls for the heavy duty tasks and using Gemini Flash free tier for the lighter tasks. That was the plan, anyway.

There are two reasons I moved away from it, the first was complexity. Different models mean different APIs, different data structures, and different approaches to prompt writing, all of which I could have solved, and so I decided that I would rather pay than increase complexity. The second reason is, after running some manual tests, I found that dropping Gemini Flash in the middle of two Sonnet calls leads to a large reduction in quality. Now looking back on it that seems pretty obvious to me, the reason you pay for a better model is for higher quality output, but the other piece that the high quality output relies on is high quality input. Flash isn't a bad model, but it just wasn't up to the tasks I was giving it, so I was stuck with low quality output that became low quality input.
So Sonnet took over everything. A cheap model still had its place. I just hadn't found it yet.

Step Functions is the textbook way to chain AWS services together, so that's what I used. Step Functions are great, and more importantly to me when I started designing this project, they have this wonderful little graphic on the AWS Console that you can screenshot and put into a blog post a lot like this one. There are a lot of reasons to use them, but here they caused a rather large headache.

LLM APIs across the board have rate limits, and for good reason, but those limits bite hard when you're not prepared for them. Step Functions lack a rather critical feature in this case, a way to limit concurrent calls, which makes a lot of sense when you consider their use cases. It just means that this project can't use them, because as much as I love following the AWS design book, the concurrency of my LLM API calls wasn't something I could leave unmanaged. I watched every job in a batch hit Anthropic at the same instant and realised something needed to change, so I switched to an orchestrator and an SQS queue to manage it.
The orchestrator doesn't make for a nice screenshot. But it does exactly what I tell it to, and nothing I don't.

I didn't want to build a frontend. I've used React before, I've written a touch of javascript, but I just don't enjoy it. Making things look nice is great and all, but I'm a function over form type of guy, and the problems on the backend of a system are just more interesting to me than the ones on the front.

Here's the thing I'd missed, though. This whole project was me using LLMs as the engine, the thing doing the work, and it never really occurred to me to use them to build the thing too. The frontend is what forced it. Designing and building a webpage from scratch isn't something I know how to do, and in 2026 I'm not sure I need to. I've got enough developer fundamentals to make sure nothing goes badly wrong when I say "Claude, build me a website."

It's more than that one sentence, of course. Mockups, writing the content so it reads how I want, checking the functionality does what it's supposed to. But the building itself, the part I'd have spent weeks fumbling through, wasn't the bottleneck anymore. The thing I'd written off as the boring part of the project turned out to be where I learned what I think might be the most important skill of the lot: not using LLMs to do the work, but using them to build the things that do.

I made a lot of assumptions when I designed this project, but the biggest was the scale. When I'm job hunting, a lot of it is scrolling past jobs I know I'm not a fit for. I can judge them at a glance and forget them right away. That forgetting is the part that matters, because when I designed this I forgot about them too. I'd apply to maybe 10 jobs in a session and assumed I'd be scraping and analysing double that. That's where I was wrong.

Once I started using this thing I wanted to do big scrapes. 50, 100, 200 jobs at a time. And all of a sudden I was out of Claude tokens. My scale was nothing like what I'd planned for, so I needed a different answer. One that kept costs down without making me open every description and decide, one by one, "yeah, that's one to look at." Back in the original design I'd thrown out the dual model approach because the quality drop was too steep, and that part is still true. A worse model in the middle of the analysis pipeline is a bad idea. But what if none of that output ever touched the pipeline.

It doesn't touch the pipeline. It comes to me instead. That's why a worse model is fine here when it wasn't before. A rough number I can glance at and overrule is useful. The same number feeding the next model is poison. So Flash came back, this time as my gut feeling. A small, tight prompt: who I am, what I bring, what I want from a job. It reads the description, leans on whatever it knows about the company, and hands me two numbers. If they look interesting I dig in, read the reasoning, decide for myself whether it's worth the full pipeline: the deep dive on the company, the real match against what I bring. And if they don't, I forget it and move on. Same as I always did, just two hundred at a time.

decisions.problem: When several jobs were scraped at once, every pipeline execution fired simultaneously off the DynamoDB stream, each making multiple Sonnet calls with nothing coordinating them. The serverless instinct here is to parallelize: company research doesn't depend on job summarisation, so the textbook move is to break it onto its own trigger and let it run concurrently. I did exactly that, and it made things worse. More independent executions meant more simultaneous LLM calls, and together they'd blow through the Anthropic rate limit within seconds. The SDK's built-in retry didn't save it either, its backoff gives up well before the rate-limit window resets, so the retries were spent long before they could land.
decisions.solution: Route every job through a single SQS queue consumed by one orchestrator Lambda, job-processor, which invokes the three analysis Lambdas sequentially within one execution, company research included. The SQS event source is capped at MaxConcurrency=2, so at most two jobs process at once no matter how many were scraped together. Collapsing the parallel work back into a sequence is the opposite of the standard advice, but the bottleneck here isn't compute, it's a shared rate limit, and you can't parallelize your way around a ceiling every call draws from the same pool. A 65-second sleep on any rate-limit error backstops the SDK's short backoff as a final guard. The tradeoff is throughput, two jobs at a time is deliberately slow, but at single-user scale the pace is invisible and the predictability is worth far more than the speed.

decisions.problem: The original plan was plain Python Lambdas with dependencies managed through Lambda layers. It held for simple cases but got fragile fast as the project grew: conflicting package versions, architecture mismatches between local development and the Lambda runtime, and layers bumping against their size limit. Every new dependency was a potential headache, and several functions share heavy libraries (the Anthropic and Google SDKs, boto3) that made the layer approach worse the more the project leaned on them.
decisions.solution: Move every Lambda to a Docker container image. Dependency management becomes an ordinary Dockerfile problem, the local and production environments match exactly because they're the same image, and the layer size ceiling stops applying. The tradeoff is slower builds, a container image takes longer to build and push than zipping a layer, but at this scale that cost is paid once per deploy and never felt at runtime.

decisions.problem: The original design used two models: Gemini Flash for the lighter tasks, Claude Sonnet for the heavy ones, split by stake to keep costs down. It was an ambitious call made early, before I'd spent much time building pipelines where one LLM's output feeds another. That's where it broke. Flash sat mid-chain, and its weaker output became the input to a Sonnet step downstream, so a single cheap link degraded everything after it. In a chain of models, the weakest link sets the ceiling for the whole thing.
decisions.solution: Consolidate the analysis pipeline onto a single model, Claude Sonnet. Quality became consistent end to end, with no weak stage handing degraded input to a stronger one downstream. Debugging got simpler, one model meant no question of which one introduced a bad output. And the codebase shed a whole parallel integration: one SDK, one credential, one prompt style instead of two. For the analysis pipeline, where every step's output is another step's input, a single strong model is the right architecture, and it still runs that way.

decisions.problem: Every scraped job ran the full analysis pipeline: summarise, company research, CV match, three Sonnet calls each. That was fine at the scale the design assumed, a few dozen jobs at a time. At the scale the tool actually got used, scrapes of 100 to 200 jobs, it was untenable. Most of those jobs were ones I'd never apply to, and they consumed the same three expensive calls as the ones I would. The cost and the rate-limit pressure both scaled with the size of the scrape, not with the number of jobs actually worth analysing.
decisions.solution: Split the work into two passes. A cheap screening pass runs Gemini Flash on every scraped job the moment it lands, producing two scores (a technical match score and a recommendation score) written straight to the job record. The expensive analysis pipeline runs only on jobs the user explicitly promotes from the dashboard. The screening output is never consumed by another model, it surfaces directly to the user as a triage signal, which is why a cheaper, lower-quality model is acceptable here. A rough score a human reads and overrules is useful, whereas a rough score feeding a downstream model degrades everything after it. This is the inverse of the earlier multi-model attempt, where Flash sat mid-pipeline and its weaker output became another model's input. Screening every job isn't free, Flash has a per-call cost at volume, but it runs about an order of magnitude cheaper than a full Sonnet pass and only the jobs worth the spend get it.

decisions.problem: The dashboard needed to group jobs into views, and the obvious cut was by pipeline state: screened jobs in one list, fully analysed jobs in another. But that tracks where a job's data is, not where the user is with it. A job mid-analysis and a job whose analysis failed are in different pipeline states but the same human state. You've decided to pursue it and you're waiting on the result. Splitting them across views by their internal status fragmented jobs that belonged together and left failed jobs cluttering the triage queue they'd already left.
decisions.solution: Define buckets by user intent rather than pipeline state. Screened means analysis has never been attempted, the untouched triage queue. Analysed means analysis has been initiated, regardless of outcome: in-flight, complete, and failed all live here, because once you've promoted a job it's left triage whether or not the pipeline has finished with it. Applied and Archive are likewise intent states, not data states. The filter logic keys off whether an action has been taken, not what the pipeline is currently doing, so a job moves between buckets when you make a decision about it, not when a Lambda changes its status.

decisions.problem: Scraped company names are messy and inconsistent. The same employer shows up as "Amazon," "Amazon Web Services," and "Amazon Support Services Pty Ltd" across different listings, and job boards often surface a recruitment agency as the advertiser instead of the actual hiring company. Treating the raw scraped name as the company identity meant the same employer fragmented into multiple records, company research ran redundantly for each variant, and culture-fit data couldn't be reused across jobs from one company.
decisions.solution: Model companies as their own entities with a resolved canonical name, separate from the raw scraped name on each job. During the screening pass, the model resolves each scraped name to a canonical public-facing brand and emits a confidence level; low confidence falls back to the raw name unchanged rather than guessing. Resolved names are matched against existing company records through a name index and a mappings table, so a new variant of a known employer attaches to the existing record instead of creating a duplicate. Every job carries a foreign key to its company record, and company research runs once per company and is reused across all its jobs, with a 30-day freshness window before it re-runs. Resolution happens in two stages: the screening pass resolves fast from the model's own knowledge with no web access, and if a job is later promoted to full analysis, company-researcher has web search and overwrites the canonical name when its better-sourced result disagrees. The cheap pass only has to get close, because the stage that actually matters corrects it.

learnings: Everyone has spent a little time messing with LLMs since they came out, but this was the first project where I sat down and actually tried to build something real with them. What surprised me was how familiar it felt. Three sentences solving the problem, fifteen making sure your edge cases are covered, your input and output formats are enforced, and the whole thing won't fall apart at the first sign of adversity. To spell it out, it felt like programming. And programming is something I know. Structured output is probably where most of the work lives. LLMs don't produce it naturally, and getting them to do it reliably means telling them three times, building guardrails, and treating every edge case like it will definitely happen. Once I started treating them like code, everything got easier. There were tips and tricks to pick up along the way, but those are just language quirks, something every programmer is familiar with. So yeah, in the end LLMs have just reinvented COBOL. But it actually works.

Here's the thing about thinking like a programmer: you inherit a programmer's instincts too, and mine run hard toward control. When you first learn to program you're taught encapsulation and modularity. Everything keeps its hands to itself, everything serves a clear purpose. That's what I built. A bunch of small prompts, specific goals, structured inputs and outputs, all talking to each other in ways I could see. Which is also when it hit me that "Job Hunt Agents" might be the wrong name for the thing I'd made. Getting into definitions in an emerging field is always messy, but in my mind an agent has a goal and maybe a little structure around how to achieve it, and then you let it do its thing. Find its own way to the answer. Now as a developer with control issues, I cannot just let my code go off and run itself. So I broke the problem into steps, each one a checkpoint where it had to stop and show its work before moving on. It works. But it's not an agent. It's an LLM powered workflow. The LLMs aren't making decisions, they aren't given agency, and so they aren't agents. Could this have been done faster with a true agent? Probably. A single prompt along the lines of "you're an expert recruiter, go find suitable jobs for this candidate" and Claude figures out the rest. But then I lose control. It becomes a black box that eats tokens and eventually spits out an answer, with minimal insight into how it got there or how to replicate it. So in the end I'm glad I didn't build an agent. I don't think an agent would give me the control I need to make sure this project works tomorrow, and next week, and next year.

explore.heading: Dashboard
explore.paragraph: Reading about it only goes so far. Go take a look. Real listings, real company research, AI-generated outputs against actual job descriptions. Browsable without a login.

explore.heading: GitHub
explore.paragraph: The full source for the pipeline, Lambda functions, and frontend. If you want to see how any of it actually works, this is the place.

nextSteps.heading: What's next
nextSteps.intro: The tool does what I need, but there are still points where it waits on me. Most of what I'd build next is about closing those gaps and letting it run further on its own.

bullets: Scheduled scrapes, so new listings show up on their own instead of waiting for me to kick off a search
Automatic retries for jobs that fail to screen, instead of sitting until I notice them

Revisiting automatic analysis. I kept it manual originally, gated behind a human checking the score, but now there's a recommendation score I trust enough to let high-scoring jobs analyse themselves above a conservative threshold

groups.intro: Past automation, a couple of extensions at the edges of the pipeline.
bullets: More job boards, since the scraper already handles multiple sources and adding another is mostly mapping its data
Built-in interview preparation, the natural stage after a job's been analysed and applied to, and the one part of the hunt the tool doesn't touch yet
